associations.item1_title: AI-Extracted Eye-Tracking Features for ADHD Screening: A Tablet-Based Approach
associations.item2_title: Unraveling ADHD Through Eye Tracking: Pupil Metrics Do Not Outperform Traditional Diagnostic Methods
associations.description: Chen et al. report 89% sensitivity for AI-extracted eye-tracking features in ADHD screening, while the Unraveling ADHD study finds pupil-based eye-tracking metrics achieve only 57% sensitivity, concluding that eye-tracking does not outperform traditional rating scales. This contradiction likely stems from different eye-tracking paradigms (AI-extracted saccadic features vs. raw pupil metrics) and different classification approaches (ML integration vs. individual metric comparison).
associations.significance: This contradiction highlights a critical methodological gap in ADHD eye-tracking research: the choice of eye-tracking features and analytical approach may determine whether the technology appears effective or not. Resolving this is essential for clinical translation.

research_gaps.description: No head-to-head comparison of AI-integrated eye-tracking features versus raw pupilometric metrics exists within a single study using the same population and apparatus.
research_gaps.evidence: Chen 2023 (AI features, positive) and Unraveling ADHD 2023 (raw pupil metrics, null) use different paradigms, populations, and analytical pipelines, making direct comparison impossible (KnowledgeItem.id=10.3389/fpsyt.2023.1260031, KnowledgeItem.id=10.3390/brainsci13050703)

research_gaps.description: Existing ADHD eye-tracking studies lack diverse population validation across different age ranges, comorbidities, and ADHD subtypes, limiting generalizability of both positive and null findings.
research_gaps.evidence: Chen 2023 samples ages 6–14 only; Unraveling ADHD 2023 does not report subtype-stratified analyses. Neither study includes ADHD-inattentive vs. combined type comparisons.

nodes.label: Unraveling ADHD Eye Tracking Pupil Metrics (2023)
